key_spectral_indices.NDRE: Early stress detection before visible symptoms appear
key_spectral_indices.EVI: Enhanced vegetation index, better in dense canopy

fertilizer_optimization.overview: Corn is the highest N-demanding arable crop. Typical UK/US target: 180-220 kg N/ha. Split applications outperform single-dose by 10-15% NUE. Always cross-reference NDVI zones for variable-rate application.

products.name: Urea
pros: Highest N concentration — cheapest N per kg
Easy to store and handle
Can be blended with other granulars
cons: Volatilisation risk if surface-applied without incorporation — can lose 15-30% N as ammonia
Needs rainfall or incorporation within 4 days
Urease inhibitor (NBPT) coating recommended in warm/dry conditions
best_for: Split spring top-dress with rain forecast
products.application_timing: Apply at pre-plant or V3-V6 side-dress. Avoid surface application >10°C without rain.

products.name: Calcium Ammonium Nitrate (CAN)
pros: Half nitrate (immediately available), half ammonium (slower release) — best of both
Lower volatilisation risk than urea
Calcium content benefits soil structure
cons: Lower N% means higher haulage and spreading cost per kg N
More expensive per kg N than urea
products.application_timing: Ideal for split top-dress at V4-V6. Can apply in drier conditions than urea.

products.name: UAN (Urea Ammonium Nitrate solution)
pros: Liquid — ideal for precision variable-rate application by GPS sprayer
Combines nitrate, ammonium and urea N forms for staggered release
Can be tank-mixed with crop protection products
cons: Leaf scorch risk if applied to wet foliage or high temperature
best_for: Fields with NDVI variability — match rate to NDVI zone
products.application_timing: Apply V4-V8 via dribble bar or injected coulters. Avoid midday high-temp foliar contact.

products.name: NPK 20-10-10
pros: Single product delivers N, P and K — reduces passes
P supports early root development
K improves drought and disease tolerance
cons: P and K rates tied to N ratio — often need supplemental N
Higher cost per kg N than straight N products
May over-apply P or K vs soil index
best_for: Fields low in P and K (soil index 0-1)
products.application_timing: Best pre-plant or placed 5cm beside/below seed at planting. Not for late top-dress.

products.name: DAP (Di-ammonium Phosphate)
pros: Very high P — best for P-deficient soils at planting
Boosts early root establishment
Granular, easy to blend
cons: Low N — needs companion N product
Not appropriate as primary N source
best_for: Starter fertilizer at planting on P-deficient soils
products.application_timing: At planting, placed or broadcast incorporated.

products.name: Muriate of Potash (MOP)
pros: Cheapest K source
High K% — low application rate needed
cons: Chloride content can affect sensitive rotations
No N or P — must be combined
products.application_timing: Pre-plant or autumn application. Avoid spring top-dress — chloride risk.

products.name: Urea + Sulphur (e.g. S-urea 38+12S)
pros: S co-applied with N — sulphur improves N use efficiency by 8-12%
S deficiency increasingly common on lighter soils post-Clean Air Act
Single pass delivers both nutrients
cons: May over-supply S on clay soils that retain atmospheric S
best_for: Sandy/light soils prone to S leaching
Where NDVI + yellowing pattern suggests S as co-limiting factor

n_rate_by_yield_target.note: Low input — drought-prone or marginal ground
Irrigated, premium hybrids — verify with soil test

split_application_plan.description: Split applications reduce volatilisation and leaching losses vs single dose. Standard 3-way split for corn:

splits.rationale: Establish P and K with starter N. Incorporated urea avoids volatilisation.
Main growth flush. Rapid uptake phase begins. N most efficiently used here.
Support tassel development and grain fill. Liquid UAN via coulter avoids leaf burn.

ndvi_to_fertilizer_action.action: No additional N needed. Maintain plan. Consider K top-up if soil index low.
Apply planned N dose at lower end of range. Scout for disease or pest before applying N — greening may be due to non-N factors.
Increase N rate by 15-20%. Apply CAN or UAN for fast availability. If uniform across field, likely N deficiency — soil test to confirm. If patchy, scout for disease.
Emergency N application — 40-60 kg N/ha CAN or UAN side-dress immediately. Check for root health / compaction / waterlogging before spending on fertilizer. ROI may be limited at this severity.
Crop likely uneconomic to rescue with N alone. Diagnose root cause (disease, flooding, compaction). Consider partial write-off and reduce remaining input spend.

Sandy loam.note: Add 10-15 kg N/ha extra — high leaching. Use 4 splits minimum.

Clay loam.note: Standard rates. Incorporation of urea easy on clays.

Silty clay.note: Risk of surface capping — CAN safer than solid urea.

Chalky loam.note: Lime-rich soils reduce P availability — ensure DAP at planting.

Peat.note: Very high N mineralisation — reduce synthetic N by 30%, test mid-season.

micronutrients.Zinc: Critical for corn — deficiency common on pH >7 soils. Symptoms: white striping on young leaves. Apply 2-5 kg Zn/ha foliar if NDVI low with striping.
micronutrients.Manganese: Deficiency on peaty/high-pH soils. Foliar Mn sulfate 1-2 kg/ha.
micronutrients.Boron: Required for pollen viability. Apply 150-200 g B/ha foliar at VT if dry weather.
micronutrients.Sulfur: Increasingly deficient on light soils. 20-30 kg S/ha alongside N. Use S-enhanced urea or separate ammonium sulfate.

diseases.name: Gray Leaf Spot
symptoms: Rectangular tan-to-gray lesions with parallel edges running along leaf veins
Yellow halo may surround lesions
Severe cases cause blighting of entire leaf
diseases.ndvi_signature: Patchy NDVI reduction (0.3-0.5) in humid areas with progressive decline
treatments: Fungicide: strobilurins (azoxystrobin, pyraclostrobin) or triazoles (propiconazole)
Apply at VT/R1 stage for maximum protection
Improve field drainage and airflow
Plant resistant hybrids next season
diseases.economic_threshold: Apply fungicide when disease present on 50% of plants at or before VT stage

diseases.name: Northern Corn Leaf Blight
symptoms: Large cigar-shaped lesions 2.5-15cm long, tan to grayish-brown
Lesions appear first on lower leaves, progress upward
Dark sporulation visible in high humidity
diseases.ndvi_signature: Rapid NDVI decline from 0.7+ to 0.3-0.4 in affected areas, starts lower canopy
treatments: Fungicide: mancozeb, propiconazole, or azoxystrobin
Best applied at early tasseling (VT) before disease spreads above ear leaf
Crop rotation with non-host crops
diseases.economic_threshold: Treat when disease reaches ear leaf before silking

diseases.name: Common Rust
symptoms: Small circular-to-elongate brick-red pustules on both leaf surfaces
Scattered randomly across leaf surface
Severe infection causes leaf yellowing and death
diseases.ndvi_signature: Diffuse, uniform NDVI reduction across field
treatments: Fungicide: triazoles most effective (propiconazole, tebuconazole)
Apply when first pustules detected before VT
Resistant hybrids rarely need treatment
diseases.economic_threshold: Treat susceptible hybrids when rust present before silking and conditions favor spread

diseases.name: Nitrogen Deficiency
symptoms: Yellowing starting at leaf tip, progressing down midrib (V-shape pattern)
Lower leaves affected first, overall pale green color
Stunted growth, thin stalks
diseases.ndvi_signature: Uniform low NDVI (0.3-0.5) across field zones, especially areas where N leaches
treatments: Side-dress UAN or CAN at V4-V8 — fastest response
Urea if incorporated or rain forecast within 24-48h
Target 40-60 kg N/ha emergency application if NDVI <0.4
Soil test to determine precise rates and split remaining season budget
diseases.economic_threshold: Apply N when NDRE indicates deficiency confirmed by soil test

fertilizer_recommendations.rationale: UAN provides immediate nitrate + slower ammonium — fastest recovery. CAN if liquid not available.

diseases.name: Drought / Water Stress
symptoms: Leaf rolling during daytime heat
Firing (brown tips) on lower leaves
Reduced growth, silking/pollen shed mismatch in severe cases
diseases.ndvi_signature: NDVI reduction proportional to stress severity, NDWI shows moisture deficit clearly
treatments: Irrigation — critical during VT-R2 (most sensitive period)
Minimum 25mm/week during grain fill
Reduce N rate to match expected lower yield potential — avoid wasted input spend
Soil moisture monitoring
diseases.economic_threshold: Irrigate when soil moisture falls below 50% of field capacity during VT-R2

diseases.name: Phosphorus Deficiency
symptoms: Purple/reddish discolouration of leaf margins and undersides
Stunted early growth — critical in first 3 weeks
Delayed maturity
diseases.ndvi_signature: Low early-season NDVI despite adequate N; purple colouration not detectable by NDVI alone
treatments: DAP or NPK starter at planting (placed 5cm beside seed)
Foliar P not highly effective — soil application is key
Warm soil to improve P uptake — deep tillage if compacted

fertilizer_recommendations.rationale: DAP delivers very high P at planting for early root establishment. If K also low, NPK blend more efficient.

diseases.name: Potassium Deficiency
symptoms: Scorch/browning of leaf margins starting on older lower leaves
Lodging — weak stalks that snap or lean over
Poor drought and disease tolerance
diseases.ndvi_signature: Marginal NDVI reduction with high spatial variability matching soil texture variation
risk_conditions: High yield crops removing >180 kg K2O/ha
treatments: MOP (Muriate of Potash 0-0-60) pre-plant at 120-180 kg/ha
If crop already growing: liquid K foliar as emergency only (limited uptake)
Long-term: build soil K index with regular MOP or compound

fertilizer_recommendations.rationale: MOP is cheapest K. Use NPK blend if N and K both needed in single pass.

poor.description: Significant stress or disease, action required
